EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper low moved across southern Quebec on February 7. This was followed by low pressure moving east from the Great Lakes on February 8. On February 9 & 10, low pressure moved off the mid-Atlantic coast becoming a noreaster as it approached southern New England. This all resulted in a long duration snow storm that dumped up to a foot and a half of snow across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to ten inches of snow fell across eastern Kent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front associated with a clipper low pressure system moving through the northeast resulted in light snow across much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell across northwest Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough set up across southern New England off a fast moving low pressure system well southeast of Nantucket. This brought accumulating snowfall to much of Rhode Island and eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of snow fell across Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance brought strong, damaging winds to southeastern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Weather Observation Station at Block Island State Airport (KBID) recorded wind gusts to 59 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed off the North Carolina coast and moved up the east coast of the United States, bringing snow to much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to seven inches of snow fell across northwestern Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed south of New England bringing snow and gusty winds to much of Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow fell across Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving over southern New England combined with a strong low level jet resulted in strong gradient winds over the region. Some of these winds were damaging, bringing down trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed onto Cross Street in Westerly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with a remnant of an elevated mixed layer (EML) and a significant amount of deep layer shear and instability to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. Many of these thunderstorms became severe, producing strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility pole was struck by lightning on North Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Bill brought tropical moisture to southern New England. This combined with strong low level forcing and some modest instability to produce showers and thunderstorms across portions of the region. While none of these storms became severe, flooding occurred with a few of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence, Valley Street was blocked because of flooding.  A car also got stuck in flood waters at the intersection of Lockwood Street and Haskins Street.  In Cranston, the intersection of Wilbur and Oaklawn Avenue was blocked because of flooding.  Several cars became stuck in flood waters at various intersections throughout the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area as a result of an upper level disturbance and a cold front.  A couple of these slow moving storms resulted in flooding or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Warren, Franklin Street flooded with water as high as a fire hydrant.  Vernon, Main, Dyer, and Washington Streets were flooded and impassable.  The Oyster Point area was flooded and impassable as well.  In Bristol, several streets were flooded and impassable, including: Gooding Avenue, Chestnut, Tupelo, Thames, and Franklin Streets, and Brook Farm Drive.  All of Thames Street was flooded in the Bristol Harbor area near Independence Park.  In Barrington, Prospect Street and Simmons Road at Massasoit Avenue as well as several other streets were flooded and impassable.  A basement was flooded on Broadview Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area as a result of an upper level disturbance and a cold front.  A couple of these slow moving storms resulted in flooding or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ocean Road, Knowles Way at Kathy Ann Drive, and Beach Street at Narragansett Avenue were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level disturbance and very cold air in the mid levels prompted showers and thunderstorms to develop, mainly across eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island.  The cold air aloft resulted in several reports of large hail in addition to some isolated tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell on East Killingly Road in Foster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level disturbance and very cold air in the mid levels prompted showers and thunderstorms to develop, mainly across eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island.  The cold air aloft resulted in several reports of large hail in addition to some isolated tree damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail covered the ground.  The hail took down some of the leaves off the trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were downed by thunderstorm winds, closing several roads around South Kingstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance sparked showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds. Others produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Barrington, New Meadow Road at Route 114 was flooded with six inches of water.  In Bristol Annawamscutt Drive at Metacom Avenue/Route 136 was closed due to flooding.  In Warren, up to one foot of water flooded Crescent, Kelly, and Water Streets and Columbus Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance sparked showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds. Others produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple cars were stuck in flood waters on Boston Neck Road in Narragansett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance sparked showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds. Others produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half an inch to quarter size hail fell in Narragansett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance sparked showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds. Others produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Main Road and knocked down a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance sparked showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds. Others produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inch diameter limbs were downed on Water Street and Columbus Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance sparked showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds. Others produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees along Old Boston Neck Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.  Large limbs were downed blocking Ocean Road.  Large limbs and wires were downed on Middlebridge Road, Juliet Road, and Old Boston Neck Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved up to southern New England from the southern plains bringing a mix of wintry precipitation to southern New England. This coincided with an arctic cold front moving through the region as well.  Due to the heavy snow collected through the month of February, the roof of the Garden Center at Lowe's on Quaker Lane in Warwick collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow fell across western Kent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved up to southern New England from the southern plains bringing a mix of wintry precipitation to southern New England. EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to five inches of snow fell across northwestern Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a cold front moved across Southern New England. This drew strong gusty northwest winds across the region during the late morning and afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at nearby T F Green Airport (KPVD) recorded sustained winds of 37 mph and gusts to 47 mph.  A sixteen-inch diameter tree limb and wires fell onto and blocked White Rock Road in Westerly. The wind brought wires down on Oshay Lane in Richmond.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to six inches of snow fell across Washington County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm brought heavy snow to southern New England with blizzard conditions to much of Rhode Island and eastern Massachusetts, beginning during the day on Monday, January 26 and lasting into the early morning hours of Tuesday, January 27.  The highest snowfall totals, averaging two to three feet, extended from extreme northeast Connecticut and northwest Rhode Island into much of central and northeast Massachusetts, including greater Boston.  Some of the highest totals reported include Hudson, MA (36 inches), Acton, MA (34 inches), Thompson, CT (33.5 inches), and Methuen, MA (31.5 inches).  Much of southeast Massachusetts and the rest of Rhode Island received one to two feet of snow.  Totals dropped off dramatically west of the Connecticut River Valley where totals of 4 to 8 inches were observed.  ||The storm was well-forecast, with Blizzard Watches and Winter Storm Watches issued 2 days before the snow began.  Low pressure tracked northeast from the Carolinas and strengthened rapidly as it slowly passed southeast of Nantucket on Monday evening, January 26.  All of the precipitation fell as snow with this storm.  At its peak, snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour were common.  In Massachusetts, blizzard conditions were officially reported in Marshfield (14 hours), Hyannis (13 hours), Nantucket (11 hours), Boston (9 hours), Chatham (9 hours), Worcester (7 hours), and Beverly (3 hours).  In Rhode Island, blizzard conditions were officially reported in Westerly (5 hours), Newport (4 hours), and at T.F. Green State Airport in Warwick (3 hours).  Several other locations fell just short of the required criteria (3 consecutive hours of blizzard conditions) including Fitchburg, New Bedford, Falmouth, and Martha���s Vineyard in Massachusetts and Smithfield in Rhode Island.||Daily snowfall records were set for January 27 in Boston (22.1 inches, previous record 8.8 inches in 2011), Worcester (31.9 inches, previous record 11.0 in 2011), and Providence (16.0 inches, previous record 6.7 inches in 2011).  In Worcester, the snowfall total of 34.5 inches was the greatest on record (dating back to 1892), breaking the previous record of 33.0 inches on March 31 to April 1, 1997.  In Providence, the total of 19.1 inches was the fourth highest on record (dating back to 1904), while in Boston the total of 24.6 inches was the sixth highest on record (dating back to 1872).||The Blizzard of January 2015 produced very strong winds late Monday into Tuesday near the Massachusetts and Rhode Island coasts where gusts of 50 to 65 mph were common.  Gusts reached hurricane force at a few locations in Massachusetts including Nantucket (78 mph), Chatham (75 mph), Humarock (74 mph), and Aquinnah (74 mph).||The Governor of Rhode Island declared a state wide travel ban beginning at midnight on January 27th and continuing through 8 pm. The few cars/drivers who did not obey the travel ban became stuck.  A RI Department of Transportation vehicle flipped over during the storm as well.||Several fatalities occurred as a result of the storm.  These included: a 68 year-old male outdoors shoveling snow on 1/27/15 and a 57 year-old male outdoors shoveling snow on 1/28/15.||President Obama issued a federal disaster declaration for the state of Rhode Island for this storm, allowing federal assistance for emergency work and repairs to facilities damaged by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions occurred at nearby T.F. Green Airport from approximately 6 am to 9 am.  Outside this time frame, near blizzard conditions occurred with gusty winds and limited visibilities.  Fourteen to twenty inches of snow fell across southeastern Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm brought heavy snow to southern New England with blizzard conditions to much of Rhode Island and eastern Massachusetts, beginning during the day on Monday, January 26 and lasting into the early morning hours of Tuesday, January 27.  The highest snowfall totals, averaging two to three feet, extended from extreme northeast Connecticut and northwest Rhode Island into much of central and northeast Massachusetts, including greater Boston.  Some of the highest totals reported include Hudson, MA (36 inches), Acton, MA (34 inches), Thompson, CT (33.5 inches), and Methuen, MA (31.5 inches).  Much of southeast Massachusetts and the rest of Rhode Island received one to two feet of snow.  Totals dropped off dramatically west of the Connecticut River Valley where totals of 4 to 8 inches were observed.  ||The storm was well-forecast, with Blizzard Watches and Winter Storm Watches issued 2 days before the snow began.  Low pressure tracked northeast from the Carolinas and strengthened rapidly as it slowly passed southeast of Nantucket on Monday evening, January 26.  All of the precipitation fell as snow with this storm.  At its peak, snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour were common.  In Massachusetts, blizzard conditions were officially reported in Marshfield (14 hours), Hyannis (13 hours), Nantucket (11 hours), Boston (9 hours), Chatham (9 hours), Worcester (7 hours), and Beverly (3 hours).  In Rhode Island, blizzard conditions were officially reported in Westerly (5 hours), Newport (4 hours), and at T.F. Green State Airport in Warwick (3 hours).  Several other locations fell just short of the required criteria (3 consecutive hours of blizzard conditions) including Fitchburg, New Bedford, Falmouth, and Martha���s Vineyard in Massachusetts and Smithfield in Rhode Island.||Daily snowfall records were set for January 27 in Boston (22.1 inches, previous record 8.8 inches in 2011), Worcester (31.9 inches, previous record 11.0 in 2011), and Providence (16.0 inches, previous record 6.7 inches in 2011).  In Worcester, the snowfall total of 34.5 inches was the greatest on record (dating back to 1892), breaking the previous record of 33.0 inches on March 31 to April 1, 1997.  In Providence, the total of 19.1 inches was the fourth highest on record (dating back to 1904), while in Boston the total of 24.6 inches was the sixth highest on record (dating back to 1872).||The Blizzard of January 2015 produced very strong winds late Monday into Tuesday near the Massachusetts and Rhode Island coasts where gusts of 50 to 65 mph were common.  Gusts reached hurricane force at a few locations in Massachusetts including Nantucket (78 mph), Chatham (75 mph), Humarock (74 mph), and Aquinnah (74 mph).||The Governor of Rhode Island declared a state wide travel ban beginning at midnight on January 27th and continuing through 8 pm. The few cars/drivers who did not obey the travel ban became stuck.  A RI Department of Transportation vehicle flipped over during the storm as well.||Several fatalities occurred as a result of the storm.  These included: a 68 year-old male outdoors shoveling snow on 1/27/15 and a 57 year-old male outdoors shoveling snow on 1/28/15.||President Obama issued a federal disaster declaration for the state of Rhode Island for this storm, allowing federal assistance for emergency work and repairs to facilities damaged by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions occurred at Newport State Airport from approximately 3 am to 7 am. Outside this time frame, near blizzard conditions occurred with gusty winds and limited visibilities.  Sixteen to nineteen inches of snow fell across Newport County.  Rhode Island's tall ship, the Continental Sloop Providence, was toppled by strong wind gusts and sustained extensive damage.  The mast was broken and the hull punctured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a cold front moved across Southern New England. This drew strong gusty northwest winds across the region during the late morning and afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at T F Green Airport (KPVD) recorded sustained winds of 37 mph and gusts to 47 mph. Two utility poles fell on a car on Diamond Hill Road in Woonsocket.  A tree fell on wires on North Road in Foster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with a remnant of an elevated mixed layer (EML) and a significant amount of deep layer shear and instability to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. Many of these thunderstorms became severe, producing strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three utility poles and tree limbs were downed onto Hill Farm Road and Town Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across southern New England combined with high precipitable waters and weak flow aloft to initiate showers and thunderstorms that produced flooding and pockets of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In North Providence, cars were stuck in flood waters on Stella Drive and Sherman Avenue.  The intersection of Newport Avenue and Ferris Avenue in East Providence was flooded and impassable.  In Pawtucket, the intersection of Conant and Weeden Street was flooded and impassable.  Route 44 in Smithfield was flooded with 6 to 12 inches of water.  In Cranston, Wilbur Avenue was impassable and two cars were stuck in floodwaters at the Oaklawn underpass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded cold front moved across southern New England, bringing both an end to widespread rainfall and producing showers and a few isolated thunderstorms. Because widespread heavy rain had occurred the previous day, additional showers and storms produced some minor street flooding. The front also brought strong pre-frontal winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two foot diameter tree was downed onto a driveway in Barrington.  The National Ocean Service (NOS) Physical Oceanographic Real Time System (PORTS) station at Conimicut Light recorded a wind gust to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed across Long Island, NY and raced toward Rhode Island. These storms caused significant wind damage across Rhode Island resulting in widespread power outages, knocking down a significant amount of trees, and causing massive traffic delays. Wind gusts were estimated to be between 60 to 80 mph, with T.F. Green Airport reporting a wind gust of 67 mph. In fact, these storms caused more power outages (121,000 customers without power) than Hurricane Sandy (107,000)! Some of the hardest hit areas were Cranston and Charlestown, RI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Coventry Hill Farm Road, Indian Trail, Maple Valley Road, and Black Rock Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed across Long Island, NY and raced toward Rhode Island. These storms caused significant wind damage across Rhode Island resulting in widespread power outages, knocking down a significant amount of trees, and causing massive traffic delays. Wind gusts were estimated to be between 60 to 80 mph, with T.F. Green Airport reporting a wind gust of 67 mph. In fact, these storms caused more power outages (121,000 customers without power) than Hurricane Sandy (107,000)! Some of the hardest hit areas were Cranston and Charlestown, RI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several police cruisers were damaged by a falling tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-five to thirty trees were downed, blocking roads in all sections of town.  The storm also toppled the Misquamicut Business Association's drive-in screen.  The screen is mounted on two metal shipping containers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southern New England bringing heavy rain, strong winds, and periods of coastal flooding along the south coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed at Perry Hill and Hazard Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southern New England bringing heavy rain, strong winds, and periods of coastal flooding along the south coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Branches and wires were downed throughout Charlestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance sparked showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds. Others produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was downed on Lindberg Avenue.  A large branch was downed onto wires on Linden Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level disturbance and very cold air in the mid levels prompted showers and thunderstorms to develop, mainly across eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island.  The cold air aloft resulted in several reports of large hail in addition to some isolated tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires on Carolina and Nooseneck Roads by thunderstorm winds.
